name: The entrapped suitors: the chaste wife tricks them into casks. The husband takes the casks to the marketplace where he opens them.
bibliographies: Italian Novella: Rotunda; India: Thompson-Balys: BENGAL . Damant Ind . Antiq . IX 12 .
additional_description: The husband takes the casks to the marketplace where he opens them.

name: The entrapped suitors: the chaste wife has them caught. Forces them to work for her.
bibliographies: Italian Novella: Rotunda; India: Thompson-Balys: PUNJAB . Bannu : Thorburn 215 .
additional_description: Forces them to work for her.

name: The entrapped suitor: tricked into room where he is left to himself.
bibliographies: Italian Novella: *Rotunda; India: Thompson-Balys: PUNJAB . Bannu : Thorburn 216 .

name: Importunate lover imprisoned and starved: later given choice of lady or food. Chooses food.
additional_description: Chooses food.

name: Lover hidden by wife in room made to fall into deep pit of treacle.
bibliographies: India: Thompson-Balys: U.P. Etah : NQ IV No. 428 .

name: Importunate lover (priest) is forced to hide in chest. Husband takes the chest to the waiting congregation. Clever priest comes out enacting the resurrection of Lazarus.
additional_description: Husband takes the chest to the waiting congregation. Clever priest comes out enacting the resurrection of Lazarus.

name: Four importunate lovers are forced to hide in four-compartmented chest which is sold.
Importunate suitor enticed into sack and beaten by husband.
Priest caught in lasso by rival lover. Mistress tells knight of priest's demands. Knight has her give assignation, and arranges around her a string lasso which he pulls, and catches priest.
additional_description: Mistress tells knight of priest's demands. Knight has her give assignation, and arranges around her a string lasso which he pulls, and catches priest.

name: Importunate suitor has his head shaved and tarred and is put into a sack and returned to his men.
To get "nothing" and to show it. Wife pushes lecherous king first into glue and then in the closet with feathers. That is "nothing" – neither bird nor man.
additional_description: Wife pushes lecherous king first into glue and then in the closet with feathers. That is "nothing"   neither bird nor man.

name: Suitor locked in pigsty.
Suitors one by one enticed into graveyard and left together. They later get revenge.
bibliographies: Type 940; Sébillot RTP IX 344, Literature orale de la Haute-Bretagne 142.
additional_description: They later get revenge.

name: Suitors as corpse, angel, and devil. First induced to lie in coffin, second to sit up with the "corpse", and the third to carry a firebrand. "Corpse" thinks others are angel and devil. All come to blows.
bibliographies: *Pauli (ed. Bolte) No. 220; Boccaccio Decameron IX No. 1 (Lee 271); Italian Novella: Rotunda.
additional_description: First induced to lie in coffin, second to sit up with the "corpse", and the third to carry a firebrand. " Corpse" thinks others are angel and devil. All come to blows.

name: Three women humiliate importunate lover. First has him hide on thorns, second has him fall into a hole, third has him fall asleep in the street. In revenge he shows them naked, except for face, to his friends. (Cf. K1213.1.)
additional_description: First has him hide on thorns, second has him fall into a hole, third has him fall asleep in the street. In revenge he shows them naked, except for face, to his friends.

name: Girl asks importunate lover for weapon to use against her father. Instead, she uses it to defend herself against the suitor.
additional_description: Instead, she uses it to defend herself against the suitor.

name: Importunate lover beaten and sent on street bearing humiliating placard.
Importunate lover tied to tree.
Importunate lover beaten with canes filled with straw. He thinks he is severely wounded.
additional_description: He thinks he is severely wounded.

name: Obscene tricks are played on repugnant simpleton who wishes to marry.
Importunate lover is given a rendezvous. Obscene tricks played on him.
additional_description: Obscene tricks played on him.

name: Wife takes lover beneath tree where she has told husband to hide. When he begins to kiss her, husband shouts "Keep a little for me." Lover, shamefaced, runs away.
additional_description: When he begins to kiss her, husband shouts "Keep a little for me. " Lover, shamefaced, runs away.

name: Woman tricks importunate lover with the head of a pike. Thereafter he thinks the vagina is toothed.
additional_description: Thereafter he thinks the vagina is toothed.

name: Mistress deceives lover with a substitute.
bibliographies: Type 1441*; Toldo Zs. f. Vksk. XIV 47; Boccaccio Decameron VIII No. 4 (Lee 254); Icelandic: Boberg; Russian: Andrejev No. 1441*; Italian Novella: *Rotunda; India: Thompson-Balys: PUNJAB . Temple Legends I 175.- MADRAS . Tamil : Kingscote 82f .; Natesa Sastri Folk - Tales 421 .

name: Bride escapes from foolish husband and leaves goat as substitute in bed.
Mistress sends man's own wife as substitute without his knowledge.
Chaste woman sends man's own wife as substitute (without his knowledge). Then the first woman's husband is substituted for the importunate lover, who has his own wife seduced.
additional_description: Then the first woman's husband is substituted for the importunate lover, who has his own wife seduced.

name: Wife (mistress) substitutes for mistress (wife). The woman has been asked for a rendezvous. She tells the suitor's wife and they exchange places.
additional_description: The woman has been asked for a rendezvous. She tells the suitor's wife and they exchange places.

name: Chaste woman substitutes corpse for herself in the bed of an insistent suitor.
King's daughter deceives king by substituting her maid.
Lover given rump to kiss. Sometimes the trick is played by a rival lover. (Chaucer's Miller's Tale).
bibliographies: *Type 1361; *F. N. Robinson Complete Works of Geoffrey Chaucer (Boston, 1933) 786b; *Bolte Frey 251 No. 89; *Barnouw "The Millers Tale van Chaucer" Handlingen van het 6. nederlandsche Philologencongres, 1910; *Thompson The Miller's Tale (Bryan and Dempster 106ff.); Italian Novella: Rotunda.
additional_description: Sometimes the trick is played by a rival lover. (Chaucer's Miller's Tale).

Lover put off till girl bathes and dresses. She escapes.

additional_description: She escapes.

bibliographies: *W. Crooke FL IX 97; Greek: *Frazer Apollodorus II 299 n. 2.
additional_description: Unwoven each night. (Penelope.)

additional_description: The girl wastes time trying them on.

additional_description: The devil brings things till the cock crows. Another girl asks for all the things at once and must dance until she dies.

additional_description: Feigns illness and then escapes.

Girl asks undesired lover to follow her but not to step on her shadow.
Girl says she has made vow not to marry until pilgrimage is made.
Girl as umpire in suitor test (shooting arrows) escapes.
Importunate lover kept overlong at supper: must leave.

bibliographies: India: Thompson-Balys: CENT . INDIA . Balaghat : Elwin Mahakoshal 93 ; Mandia : Elwir Mahakoshal 76 .

additional_description: Knowing that he has deceived another by paying her with gilded coin she answers: "You will have to pay in better coin than is your wont!

bibliographies: *Pauli (ed. Bolte) No. 749; Heptameron No. 27; Irish myth: Cross; Italian Novella: *Rotunda.

name: Trickster deceives dupe into killing his own children.
section: K1460. Members of dupe's family killed.

name: Blinded slave's revenge. Threatens to jump from tower with lord's children unless lord blinds himself. Lord does so but slave jumps with children nevertheless.
section: K1460. Members of dupe's family killed.

name: Master's mother killed: wood heaved on her head.
section: K1460. Members of dupe's family killed.

name: [First Edition: K1490. Dupe's property destroyed – miscellaneous.]
section: K1460. Members of dupe's family killed.

name: Deception connected with adultery.
bibliographies: Polynesia: Kirtley: Societies: Caillot, 1914, 119f.; Hawaii: Fornander, 1916, IV, 100; Samoa: Krämer, 1902, 128f.

name: Cuckold. Husband deceived by adulterous wife.
additional_description: Husband deceived by adulterous wife.

name: Solomon as cuckold.
Cuckold feigns to be asleep when paramour calls.
Adulteress outwits husband.
bibliographies: *Penzer V 106 n. 1; *Bolte Frey 223f. No. 21; *Hollander MLN XXVII 71; Irish myth: *Cross; Icelandic: Boberg; India: Thompson-Balys.

name: Adulteress kills home-coming husband.
Wife of philanderer gets revenge by having an affair herself.

The husband locked out. An adulteress returns home late at night and her husband refuses to admit her. She threatens to throw herself into the well. The husband goes after her. She enters the house and bars him out.
bibliographies: *Type 1377; *Basset 1001 Contes II 128; *Wesselski Hodscha Nasreddin II 185 No. 350; *Campbell Sages xc (Puteus); Chauvin VIII 184 No. 224, IX 23; Alphabet No. 538; Hdwb. d. Märchens I 95b; Dunlop-Wilson II 111f.; Boccaccio Decameron VII No. 4; Spanish Exempla: Keller; Italian Novella: *Rotunda.
additional_description: An adulteress returns home late at night and her husband refuses to admit her. She threatens to throw herself into the well. The husband goes after her. She enters the house and bars him out.

name: Adulteress refuses to admit husband under pretence that he is a stranger.
bibliographies: *Pauli (ed. Bolte) No. 678; Cent Nouvelles Nouvelles No. 1.

name: The cut-off nose. (Lai of the Tresses.) A woman leaves her husband's bed and has another woman take her place. The husband addresses her, gets no answer and cuts off her nose (hair). In the morning the wife still has her nose (hair). The husband is made to believe that it has grown back by a miracle (or that he was dreaming).
bibliographies: *Type 1417; Bédier Fabliaux 228ff.; Chauvin VI 100 No. 267; *Penzer V 47 n. 3, 223ff., VI 271; Mensa Philosophica No. 40; Boccaccio Decameron VIII No. 8 (Lee 222); Cent Nouvelles Nouvelles No. 38; Bødker Exempler 280 No. 24; Spanish Exempla: Keller; Italian Novella: *Rotunda; India: Thompson-Balys: U.P. Oudh : Crooke Ind . Antiq . XXXV 146 No. 8.-
additional_description: (Lai of the Tresses.) A woman leaves her husband's bed and has another woman take her place. The husband addresses her, gets no answer and cuts off her nose (hair). In the morning the wife still has her nose (hair). The husband is made to believe that it has grown back by a miracle (or that he was dreaming).

name: Cut-off finger proves wife's chastity. A chaste wife substitutes a maidservant for seducer. A finger and ring are cut off as proof of wife's unfaithfulness (chastity wager with husband). Refuted by husband, who knows they are not his wife's.
bibliographies: Cf. Type 882; Köhler-Bolte I 375; Child V 497 s. v. "Substitution"; Wesselski Märchen 213 No. 19; Irish myth: Cross; Welsh: MacCulloch Celtic 110.
additional_description: A chaste wife substitutes a maidservant for seducer. A finger and ring are cut off as proof of wife's unfaithfulness (chastity wager with husband). Refuted by husband, who knows they are not his wife's.

name: The wife's equivocal oath. A husband insists that his wife take oath that she has been intimate with no one but himself. The paramour masks as ass-driver. She hires an ass from him, falls down, and lets him pick her up. She then swears that no one has touched her except her husband and the ass-driver.
bibliographies: *Type 1418; *Basset 1001 Contes II 4; **J. J. Meyer Isoldes Gottesurteil in seiner erotischen Beziehung (Berlin, 1914); *Pauli (ed. Bolte) No. 206; Rohde Der griechische Roman 484; BP IV 154, 387f.; Hdwb. d. Abergl. II 669; *Schoepperle I 225f.; Hertel Zs. f. Vksk. XVIII 385. Icelandic: Boberg; Italian Novella: *Rotunda; India: Thompson-Balys: KASHMIR . Stein- Grierson 29.-U.P. Mirzapur : NQ III No. 413 , V No. 139. BALUCHISTAN . Balochi : Dames Folk - Lore IV 291 .; Chinese: Chavannes 500 Contes I 387 No. 116.
additional_description: A husband insists that his wife take oath that she has been intimate with no one but himself. The paramour masks as ass-driver. She hires an ass from him, falls down, and lets him pick her up. She then swears that no one has touched her except her husband and the ass-driver.

name: Adulteress gets rid of husband while she entertains lover.
bibliographies: Icelandic: Boberg; India: Thompson-Balys: U.P. Oudh : Crooke Ind . Antiq . XXXV 179 No. 14 .

name: Wife has hiding husband carried off in basket by thieves . ASSAM HILLS . Kachari : Anderson 55f.
bibliographies: India: Thompson-Balys Polynesia: Kirtley: Rennell: Elbert-Monberg, 1964, No. 196.

name: The husband in the chicken house. The husband returns unexpectedly and surprises his wife with her lover. She makes the husband believe he is pursued and hides him in the chicken house. (Cf. K1514.9.)
bibliographies: *Type 1419A; **Schofield Sources and History of the 7th Novel of the 7th Day in the Decameron (Harvard Studies and Notes II); Bédier Fabliaux 450; Boccaccio Decameron III No. 4 (Lee 213); Cent Nouvelles Nouvelles No. 88; Italian Novella: *Rotunda; India: Thompson-Balys: CEYLON . Sinhalese : Orientalist II 148
additional_description: The husband returns unexpectedly and surprises his wife with her lover. She makes the husband believe he is pursued and hides him in the chicken house.

name: Husband duped into doing penance while rascal enjoys the wife.
bibliographies: Boccaccio Decameron III No. 4 (Lee 75); Italian Novella: *Rotunda.

name: Husband duped into believing he is in purgatory.
bibliographies: Boccaccio Decameron III No. 4 (Lee 91); Italian Novella: *Rotunda.

name: Returning husband beaten by servants. Mistaken for lover whom he has told them to beat.
bibliographies: Von der Hagen II xiii No. 27.
additional_description: Mistaken for lover whom he has told them to beat.

name: Husband beaten by paramour. Husband, dressed in wife's clothing, is beaten by forewarned paramour. The latter says that he is testing the wife's fidelity to her husband.
additional_description: Husband, dressed in wife's clothing, is beaten by forewarned paramour. The latter says that he is testing the wife's fidelity to her husband.

name: Husband hides in curtain to catch paramour. On entering, paramour threatens to kill husband if he should appear.
additional_description: On entering, paramour threatens to kill husband if he should appear.

name: Cuckold husband hides under bed.
Husband hides in chest to catch paramour. (Cf. K1566.)
bibliographies: Cent Nouvelles Nouvelles No. 27; Italian Novella: *Rotunda.

name: Adulteress locks up hidden husband and meets lovers.
Wife has hiding husband carried off in basket by thieves.
Wife throws husband down precipice so she can be with lover.
Adulteress has lover unload wood on doorstep. This keeps husband out.
additional_description: This keeps husband out.

name: Adulteress sets husband to watch for intruder while she entertains the paramour.
Illness feigned to call physician paramour.
bibliographies: Italian Novella: Rotunda; India: Thompson-Balys: PUNJAB . Temple Legends II 560

name: Adulteress pretends to go to say her prayers. Keeps tryst with paramour.
additional_description: Keeps tryst with paramour.

name: Adulteress gives paramour tryst in house of ill-fame. Meets husband who leaves in shame.
additional_description: Meets husband who leaves in shame.

name: Paramour unties mare. Husband chases mare while the wife entertains the paramour.
additional_description: Husband chases mare while the wife entertains the paramour.

name: Adulteress throws small coffer out of window. While the husband retrieves it the paramour changes hiding places.
additional_description: While the husband retrieves it the paramour changes hiding places.

name: Lover masks as pregnant woman: adulteress sent by husband to act as midwife, meets lover.
Adulteress together with lover while husband sleeps.
Wife drugs husband and visits paramour.
bibliographies: India: Thompson-Balys: MADRAS . Natesa Sastri Folk-

name: Husband frightened into sleeping alone. Adulteress has servants impersonate demons.
additional_description: Adulteress has servants impersonate demons.

name: Adulteress makes excuse to go and attend to bodily needs: meets lover.

The animal in the chest. The husband has locked the surprised paramour in a chest while he fetches his family as witness of his wife's unfaithfulness. She frees the lover, substitutes an animal, and discountenances the husband. (Cf. K1542, K1555, K1566, K1574.)
bibliographies: *Type 1419B; *Wesselski Hodscha Nasreddin II 187 No. 363; Cent Nouvelles Nouvelles No. 61; Italian Novella: *Rotunda.
additional_description: The husband has locked the surprised paramour in a chest while he fetches his family as witness of his wife's unfaithfulness. She frees the lover, substitutes an animal, and discountenances the husband.

name: The husband's good eye covered. The wife holds a cloth in front of his one good eye, so that he cannot see the paramour.
bibliographies: *Type 1419C; *Bédier Fabliaux 119, 466; *Chauvin IX 20 Nos. 7, 8; *Wesselski Märchen 187 No. 2; *Jellinek Euphorion IX 162f.; Alphabet No. 536; Hdwb. d. Märchens I 94b; Scala Celi 86b No. 505; Oesterley No. 123; von der Hagen II xxixff.; Dunlop-Wilson II 13; Heptameron No. 6; Cent Nouvelles Nouvelles No. 16; *Krappe Bulletin Hispanique XXXIX 27; Italian Novella: *Rotunda.
additional_description: The wife holds a cloth in front of his one good eye, so that he cannot see the paramour.

name: The husband's good eye treated. The wife pretends to treat his one good eye, so that he cannot see the paramour.
bibliographies: *Chauvin IX 20 Nos. 7, 8; Alphabet No. 535; Scala Celi 86b No. 504; Oesterley No. 122; Hitopadesa (ed. Morley) 66; Mensa Philosophica No. 49; *Krappe Bulletin Hispanique XXXIX 27; Spanish Exempla: Keller.
additional_description: The wife pretends to treat his one good eye, so that he cannot see the paramour.

name: Physician treating man's eye covers his one good eye. Entertains his patient's mistress.
bibliographies: Cent Nouvelles Nouvelles No. 87; Italian Novella: *Rotunda.
additional_description: Entertains his patient's mistress.

name: Girl covers nurse's one good eye so that she cannot see her lover.
Adulteress extinguishes light to hide her paramour.
Adulteress covers husband's eyes during incantation. Meanwhile paramour escapes.
additional_description: Meanwhile paramour escapes.

name: Adulteress persuades husband to milk cow with his eyes blindfolded: meets lover. (Cf. Chaucer's Merchant's Tale.)
The faithless wife asks her gullible husband how he would act if he were blind. The husband closes his eyes; meanwhile the lover escapes.
additional_description: The husband closes his eyes; meanwhile the lover escapes.

name: Wife washes husband's hair hiding his eyes while lover escapes unseen.
Wife has husband look for hole in pot she has bought, allowing lover to escape unseen.
Wife shows husband her milk-filled breasts and squirts milk in his eyes allowing lover to escape unseen.
Paramour escapes by disguise.
Escaping paramour said to be a deity . CEYLON . Parker I 223.

The lovers as pursuer and fugitive. The wife is visited by two gallants. When the husband approaches, one goes out with drawn sword; the other hides in the house. She convinces her husband that she has given refuge to a fugitive.
bibliographies: *Type 1419D; *Bédier Fabliaux 229ff.; *Basset 1001 Contes II 143; *Wesselski Hodscha Nasreddin II 186 No. 351; *Chauvin VIII 39 No. 7, IX 21 No. 8; Boccaccio Decameron VII No. 6 (Lee 203); von der Hagen II xxxii ff; Dunlop-Wilson II 114ff.; Hdwb. d. Märchens I 99b. Spanish Exempla: Keller; Italian Novella: *Rotunda; India: Thompson-Balys: INDIA . Hertel 60 .
additional_description: The wife is visited by two gallants. When the husband approaches, one goes out with drawn sword; the other hides in the house. She convinces her husband that she has given refuge to a fugitive.

name: One lover disguised and carried out of house by other. The wife has the lover put on a robe and stand in the corner; she tells the husband that a tradesman has left the robe and will return for it. The other lover comes and she gives him the "robe".
additional_description: The wife has the lover put on a robe and stand in the corner; she tells the husband that a tradesman has left the robe and will return for it. The other lover comes and she gives him the "robe".

name: Paramour poses as doctor.
bibliographies: Boccaccio Decameron VII No. 3 (Lee 189); Italian Novella: Rotunda.

name: Paramour in vat: disguise as vat-buyer. Husband thinks he is testing the vat.
bibliographies: Boccaccio Decameron VII No. 2 (Lee 186); Italian Novella: *Rotunda.
additional_description: Husband thinks he is testing the vat.

name: Lover hidden in chest with feathers. Husband believes he is a devil. (Cf. K1218.1.)
bibliographies: J. Prinz "A Tale of a Prioress and her Three Wooers" Literar.-Hist. Forschungen XLVII (Berlin, 1912) 65ff., 113ff.; Hilka Compilatio Singularis Exemplorum No. 14.
additional_description: Husband believes he is a devil.

name: Paramour falls in cesspool. Husband thinks he is a demon.
bibliographies: Cent Nouvelles Nouvelles No. 72; Italian Novella: *Rotunda.
additional_description: Husband thinks he is a demon.

name: Paramour poses as unsuccessful suitor. When surprised with the wife he tells the husband that he has been trying to force the woman, with no success. The wife supports the statement.
additional_description: When surprised with the wife he tells the husband that he has been trying to force the woman, with no success. The wife supports the statement.

name: Paramour escapes disguised as monk.
Paramour disguised as pregnant woman.
Paramour poses as robber.
Paramour disguised as cloth merchant is surprised by the husband. He asks the woman to be paid for a pretended sale.
additional_description: He asks the woman to be paid for a pretended sale.

name: Paramour leaving love-tryst is met by husband. Pretends he had come to see him on business.
additional_description: Pretends he had come to see him on business.

name: Paramour escapes by pretending to be returning borrowed basket.
Escaping paramour said to be a deity.
The enchanted pear tree. The wife makes the husband, who has seen the adultery from the tree, believe that the tree is magic or that he has seen double.

Robinson Complete Works of Geoffrey Chaucer (Boston, 1933) 817 (Merchant's Tale); *Basset 1001 Contes II 150ff.; Chauvin VIII 98 No. 69, IX 39 No. 34; *Bédier Fabliaux 468; *Stiefel Zs. f. Vksk. additional_description: The wife makes the husband, who has seen the adultery from the tree, believe that the tree is magic or that he has seen double.

name: Husband who has surprised wife and paramour is made to believe that he has had an illusion.
Paramour successfully hidden from husband.
bibliographies: *Type 1364; *Wesselski Märchen 187 No. 2; Italian Novella: *Rotunda; India: Thompson-Balys.

name: Paramour successfully hidden in chimney (fireplace).
Paramour successfully hidden in chest.
Paramour placed in chest and covered with clothing.
Paramour hidden under the wash.
Paramour hidden in the bed.
Wife hides lover under bed. When husband comes in she drops candle and sends him out for another, allowing lover to escape unseen.
additional_description: When husband comes in she drops candle and sends him out for another, allowing lover to escape unseen.

name: Paramour hidden behind a screen.
Lover escapes behind the sheet which wife holds up to show her husband.
Husband busied with performing task while paramour escapes.
Paramour rolled into a carpet.
bibliographies: India: Thompson-Balys: PUNJAB . Swynnerton Upper Indus 259 .

name: Husband in hanging tub to escape coming flood. The priest who has thus duped the husband enjoys the wife.
bibliographies: *Type 1361; *F. N. Robinson Complete Works of Geoffrey Chaucer (Boston 1933) 786b (Miller's Tale); *Hammond Chaucer: a Bibliographical Manual 275; *Barnouw "The Miller's Tale van Chaucer" Handlingen van het 6. nederlandsche Philologencongres (1910), Mod. Lang. Rev. VII 145; *Thompson in Bryan and Dempster 106ff.
additional_description: The priest who has thus duped the husband enjoys the wife.

name: Underground passage to paramour's house. (Inclusa.) Woman goes from one to the other. Her husband is made to believe that the woman next door is her sister.
bibliographies: *Fischer-Bolte 219; Wesselski Märchen 188 No. 2; *Chauvin V 213 No. 121, VIII 96 No. 67; *Köhler-Bolte I 393; Campbell Sages cx; *Krappe Archivum Romanicum XIX (1935) 213–226; Cent Nouvelles Nouvelles No. 1; Icelandic: *Boberg; Italian Novella: *Rotunda; India: Thompson-Balys: CENT . INDIA . Balaghat : Elwin
additional_description: (Inclusa.) Woman goes from one to the other. Her husband is made to believe that the woman next door is her sister.

name: Adulteress falls in mud at lover's door. She deceives her husband by saying that she must enter and clean her dress.
bibliographies: *Crane Vitry 226f. No. 230; Herbert III 18; *Wesselski Mönchslatein 25 No. 19; Mensa Philosophica No. 70; Italian Novella: *Rotunda.
additional_description: She deceives her husband by saying that she must enter and clean her dress.

name: The Lord above; the lord below. A husband returning home surprises a woman and her paramour and a numskull who has blundered in. The woman hides the numskull in the bed and the paramour under it. The husband, who is leaving on a journey, lifts his hands to heaven and says, "I commend you to the Lord above." – The numskull: "Commend her rather to the lord below!"
bibliographies: *Wesselski Hodscha Nasreddin I 271 n. 1, Bebel II 99 No. 2, Morlini 286 No. 30; *Bédier Fabliaux 453; Rumanian: Schullerus FFC LXXVIII No. 1380 II*; Cent Nouvelles Nouvelles No. 34; Italian Novella: Rotunda.
additional_description: A husband returning home surprises a woman and her paramour and a numskull who has blundered in. The woman hides the numskull in the bed and the paramour under it. The husband, who is leaving on a journey, lifts his hands to heaven and says, "I commend you to the Lord above. "   The numskull: "Commend her rather to the lord below!

name: Friar's trousers on adulteress's bed: relic to cure sickness. The husband is duped into believing that the friar has come to visit the sick.
additional_description: The husband is duped into believing that the friar has come to visit the sick.

name: The feigned wedding-feast. The husband returns unexpectedly to find his wife entertaining the paramour with a sumptuous feast. He is made to believe the feast is in honor of some newly-weds.
additional_description: The husband returns unexpectedly to find his wife entertaining the paramour with a sumptuous feast. He is made to believe the feast is in honor of some newly-weds.

name: Wife confesses to disguised husband. She suspects the fraud and persuades him that she knew the ruse and was testing him. He begs forgiveness.
additional_description: She suspects the fraud and persuades him that she knew the ruse and was testing him. He begs forgiveness.

name: Husband transformed to goat must witness wife's adultery. The devil has let him see his wife's unfaithfulness in this way.
bibliographies: Type 824*; Lithuanian: Balys Index No. 824*; Estonian: Aarne FFC XXV No. 824*; Russian: Andrejev No. 824*.
additional_description: The devil has let him see his wife's unfaithfulness in this way.

name: Gullible husband under the bed.
Adulteress tells her lover how she loves her husband. She thus deceives the spying husband under the bed.
bibliographies: Penzer V 108 n. 2; Bødker Exempler 269 No. 59; Spanish Exempla: Keller; Italian Novella: Rotunda.
additional_description: She thus deceives the spying husband under the bed.

name: Adulteress tells how she may save her husband's life. Discovering him under the bed, she tells lover that at temple she has learned that her husband is to die soon unless she prevents death by sleeping with a strange man. The husband is satisfied.
bibliographies: Benfey Panchatantra II 258ff., Panchatantra III 12 (tr. Ryder 348).
additional_description: Discovering him under the bed, she tells lover that at temple she has learned that her husband is to die soon unless she prevents death by sleeping with a strange man. The husband is satisfied.

name: Gullible husband behind the tree. (Tristan and Isolt.) Husband goes to wife's love tryst and hides behind a tree. The wife, having learned of his presence, tells lover that he should not allow their innocent relations to lead to gossip. Husband is appeased.
additional_description: (Tristan and Isolt.) Husband goes to wife's love tryst and hides behind a tree. The wife, having learned of his presence, tells lover that he should not allow their innocent relations to lead to gossip. Husband is appeased.

name: Adulteress transforms her husband into an animal to get rid of him. (The Tsar's Dog.)
bibliographies: Type 449*; Malone PMLA XLIII 418, 421, 441; Chauvin V 3, 5f., 198, *VI 198, VII 129f.; Penzer III 194; Kittredge Arthur 246ff.; Anderson Roman apuleja i narodnaja skazka I 376–487, 612–633; Lithuanian: Balys Index No. 3655; Russian: Andrejev No. 499A*; India: Thompson-Balys: HIMALAYA . Simla : Dracott 179. - PUNJAB . Folk - Lore XXXIII 211ff .
additional_description: (The Tsar's Dog.)

name: Adulteress transforms man to stone up to the waist.
Woman has husband made monk while he is drunk, so as to get rid of him.
bibliographies: Scala Celi 87a No. 506; Crane Vitry No. 231; Liebrecht 124; *Krappe Bulletin Hispanique XXXIX 38; Spanish Exempla: Keller.

name: Death feigned to meet paramour. Meetings in the grave (grave box).
bibliographies: *Wesselski Märchen 197; *Child V 3f., 6, 280; *Pauli (ed. Bolte) No. 876; N. Am. Indian: *Thompson Tales 305 n. 109t.
additional_description: Meetings in the grave (grave box).

name: Wife feigns death and slips out to lover.
Death feigned so man can live with mistress.

Sexton's own wife brings her offering. The priest grants to the sexton the offerings brought by all women whom the priest has loved. The priest always calls out "Take" when these women offer. The sexton's own wife comes. The priest calls out "Take!" (Cf. Q384.)
additional_description: The priest grants to the sexton the offerings brought by all women whom the priest has loved. The priest always calls out "Take" when these women offer. The sexton's own wife comes. The priest calls out "Take!

name: Husband frightened by wife's paramour in hog pen. The husband sees the paramour who has hidden in the pen and says, "Who are you?" "I am a miserable hog." The husband thinks that his hogs are possessed. (Cf. K1515, K1555, K1566, K1574.)
bibliographies: Wesselski Bebel I 206 No. 92; Spanish: Espinosa III No. 193.
additional_description: The husband sees the paramour who has hidden in the pen and says, "Who are you? " "I am a miserable hog. " The husband thinks that his hogs are possessed.

name: The marked coat in the wife's room. A procuress obtains a woman for her client by leaving a marked coat in her room. The husband drives the wife away and she joins her lover. The procuress then goes to the husband and alleges that she lost a coat with certain marks. The husband is deceived and takes the wife back.
bibliographies: *Bédier Fabliaux 443; **Eberling Auberée, altfranzösische fabel etc. (Berlin, 1891); Spanish Exempla: Keller.
additional_description: A procuress obtains a woman for her client by leaving a marked coat in her room. The husband drives the wife away and she joins her lover. The procuress then goes to the husband and alleges that she lost a coat with certain marks. The husband is deceived and takes the wife back.

name: Husband unwittingly instrumental in wife's adultery. (Usually shares his bedmate with others, not knowing that she is his wife.)
bibliographies: Heptameron No. 8; Cent Nouvelles Nouvelles No. 9; Spanish Exempla: Keller; Italian Novella: *Rotunda.
additional_description: (Usually shares his bedmate with others, not knowing that she is his wife.)

name: Husband rescues wife's paramour. Not knowing lover's identity, husband takes him to his wife and entrusts him to her care. He then leaves on a trip.
additional_description: Not knowing lover's identity, husband takes him to his wife and entrusts him to her care. He then leaves on a trip.

name: Wives wager as to who can best fool her husband.
bibliographies: *Type 1406; *Liebrecht 124; Bédier Fabliaux 265ff.; *Crane Vitry 227 No. 231, cf. 238 No. 248; Boccaccio Decameron VII No. 9 (Lee 231); Christensen DF XLVII 229; Italian Novella: *Rotunda.

name: Woman warns lover of husband by parody incantation. (Cf. K1961.1.2.1, V66.1, X441.)
bibliographies: Boccaccio Decameron VII No. 1 (Lee 185); Italian Novella: Rotunda.

name: Woman warns lover of husband by singing song.
Woman encourages paramour by song.
bibliographies: India: Thompson-Balys: PUNJAB . Swynnerton Upper Indus 260

name: Adulteress makes believe that her suspicious husband is insane. He is taken away.
additional_description: He is taken away.

name: Adulteress outwits husband – miscellaneous motifs.
Woman has paramour steal her husband's clothes. Paramour gains entrance disguised as the husband. The husband without his clothes is driven away from his home.
additional_description: Paramour gains entrance disguised as the husband. The husband without his clothes is driven away from his home.

name: Wife surprised in adultery feigns death. Comes "back to life" on hearing husband say he has seen nothing. (Cf. K1538.)
additional_description: Comes "back to life" on hearing husband say he has seen nothing.

name: Lover carried away on mistress's shoulders so that his footprints will not be visible in the snow.
Lover leaves horse outside house as husband comes up: wife tells husband their cow has foaled a horse.
bibliographies: India: Thompson-Balys. Cf. English, Scottish: *Child No. 274.

name: Unfaithful wife communicates with lover by pouring milk into stream.
Wife's attendants on trip chase wrong man as suspected lover and miss real lover.
Husband deceived as to noise made by hidden paramour.
Woman cooks food for paramour.
Husband outwits adulteress and paramour.
section: K1550. Husband outwits adulteress and paramour.
bibliographies: India: Thompson-Balys: CENT . INDIA . Elwin Myths

name: Husband discovers wife's adultery.
section: K1550. Husband outwits adulteress and paramour.

name: Adulteress detected: husband secretly drops dye on her dress.
section: K1550. Husband outwits adulteress and paramour.

section: K1550. Husband outwits adulteress and paramour.

name: Adulteress detected by food she prepares for paramour.
section: K1550. Husband outwits adulteress and paramour.

name: Husband returns home secretly and spies on adulteress and lovers.
section: K1550. Husband outwits adulteress and paramour.

name: Sham dumb man wins suit. The trickster meets a man in a narrow place in the road and calls out to him to make room. The man refuses and the trickster turns over the cart. In court the trickster plays dumb. The plaintiff says, "He is not dumb; he called out to me several times to get out of the way." Damages are assessed against the plaintiff for negligence.

additional_description: Ogre overawed.

Bluff: harrow said to be comb of hero's mother.

additional_description: Frightens wolf.

name: The bear trainer and his bear. (Schrätel und Wasserbär.) Ogre is driven out by hero's bear. The next year the ogre asks, "Is the big cat still living?" Hero says that it now has many kittens. Ogre is overawed.
bibliographies: *Type 1161; **Taylor MPh XVII 305ff.; **Bolte Zs. f. Vksk. XXXIII–XXXIV 33ff.; Christiansen "Kjætten paa Døvre" Videnskapsselskapets Skrifter 2 kl. (1922) No. 6; *Fb "hund" I 678b; Kristensen Danske Sagn I (1892) 434ff., (1928) 291–92; Scotland: Baughman.
additional_description: (Schrdtel und Wasserbdr.) Ogre is driven out by hero's bear. The next year the ogre asks, "Is the big cat still living? " Hero says that it now has many kittens. Ogre is overawed.

name: Wages: as much as he can carry. To get rid of the boy the troll offers him as large wages as he is able to carry. Boy says that this will be too much, that he will be contented merely with what the troll can carry.
additional_description: To get rid of the boy the troll offers him as large wages as he is able to carry. Boy says that this will be too much, that he will be contented merely with what the troll can carry.

name: Ogre made to believe hero has withstood fire. Hero escapes and after the room he has been in is burned he returns and is found sitting in the ashes. "It was a bit hot," he says.
additional_description: Hero escapes and after the room he has been in is burned he returns and is found sitting in the ashes. " It was a bit hot," he says.

name: Giant persuaded that hero has pushed hole in wall with bare hand. Hole bored before.
additional_description: Hole bored before.

name: Dog pretends to be calling dog in the moon when he barks.
Troll bluffed away from christening. He is invited but told that guests will include the Virgin Mary, Thor the Thunderer, etc. He stays away but sends the finest present.
bibliographies: *Type 1165; Swedish, Norwegian, Danish, German, Finnish, Estonian, Livonian, Latvian, Lithuanian: *Balys Tautosakos Darbai VI 137–161.
additional_description: He is invited but told that guests will include the Virgin Mary, Thor the Thunderer, etc. He stays away but sends the finest present.

name: Bluff: hero professes to be able to perform much larger task than that assigned.
Felling the whole forest. Told to bring in a tree, the hero asks, "Why not the whole forest?" The ogre is frightened.
bibliographies: *Type 1049; *BP III 333; Spanish: Espinosa III Nos. 163–167; India: Thompson-Balys: CEYLON . Parker II 255
additional_description: Told to bring in a tree, the hero asks, "Why not the whole forest? " The ogre is frightened.

name: Bluff: told to bring home a tree, hero prepares to bring home six.
A thousand at one shot. Told to shoot one or two wild boars, hero asks, "Why not a thousand at one shot?" The ogre is frightened.
additional_description: Told to shoot one or two wild boars, hero asks, "Why not a thousand at one shot? " The ogre is frightened.

name: Bluff: told to bring home an ox, hero prepares to bring home ten.
Bringing the whole well. Told to get water, hero demands bucket large enough to bring in the whole well. The ogre is frightened.
bibliographies: Type 1049; *BP III 333; Spanish: Espinosa III Nos. 163–167.
additional_description: Told to get water, hero demands bucket large enough to bring in the whole well. The ogre is frightened.

name: Bluff: told to bring water in an ox skin, hero prepares to dig a canal.
Wrestler claims to be able even to carry away a mountain.
bibliographies: India: Thompson-Balys: MADRAS . Telugu : Pantulu Telegus 7 .

name: Hero threatens to pull the lake together with a rope. The ogre is intimidated.
bibliographies: *Types 1045, 1650; Spanish: Espinosa Nos. 163–167; Missouri French: Carrière.
additional_description: The ogre is intimidated.

name: Hero threatens to haul away the warehouse with a rope. The ogre is intimidated.
additional_description: The ogre is intimidated.

name: Trickster threatens to throw weight into a cloud: ogre intimidated.
Ogre terrified by woman's legs. He has formerly been caught in a vise. On his approach, the man's wife stands on her head and ogre thinks her legs are a vise. He flees.
bibliographies: *Type 1159; *BP II 530 n. 3. Cf. Lithuanian: Balys Index No. 1154*, 1164C*.
additional_description: He has formerly been caught in a vise. On his approach, the man's wife stands on her head and ogre thinks her legs are a vise. He flees.

name: Bear frightened away by man threatening to cleave its skull with his penis. He meets a woman who, upon being told what man had threatened, shows him a vestige of the cleaving she once got. Only partly healed.
bibliographies: India: Thompson-Balys: KASHMIR . Buru- shaski : Lorimer 230f .
additional_description: He meets a woman who, upon being told what man had threatened, shows him a vestige of the cleaving she once got. Only partly healed.

name: Ogre terrified by an iron man. In order to save the king's daughter from the ogre an iron man is forged.
additional_description: In order to save the king's daughter from the ogre an iron man is forged.

name: Other bluffs.
Bluff: provisions for the swimming match. In a swimming match from a ship the hero takes a knapsack of provisions on his back. His rival is afraid and gives up.
bibliographies: *Type 1612; N. Am. Indian (Maliseet): Speck JAFL XXX 482 No. 7; Cape Verde Islands: Parsons MAFLS XV (1) 190.
additional_description: In a swimming match from a ship the hero takes a knapsack of provisions on his back. His rival is afraid and gives up.

name: Bluff: climbing the mast. In a contest in climbing the mast the hero falls into the rigging. "You do the same thing," he challenges. The sailors are persuaded of his expertness.
bibliographies: *Type 1611; N. Am. Indian (Maliseet): Speck JAFL XXX 482 No. 7; Cape Verde Islands: Parsons MAFLS XV (1) 190.
additional_description: In a contest in climbing the mast the hero falls into the rigging. " You do the same thing," he challenges. The sailors are persuaded of his expertness.

name: Bluff in court: the stone in the purse. A poor man has a stone in his purse to throw at the judge if he is sentenced. The judge thinks that he has money to use as a bribe and acquits him.
additional_description: A poor man has a stone in his purse to throw at the judge if he is sentenced. The judge thinks that he has money to use as a bribe and acquits him.

name: Trickster's boasting scares his powerful opponent from contest.
bibliographies: India: Thompson-Balys: ASSAM VALLEY . Borooah 115f . No. 15 .

name: False boasting of having killed his foster-brother makes his men follow the boaster.
Goat singing a threatening song bought off with food and jewels.
bibliographies: India: Thompson-Balys: U.P . Crooke - Rouse 211 No. 29 .

name: Bluffing threat.
Sham threat: "In earnest or in jest?" A man asks another who has brushed against him: "Did you do that in earnest or in jest?" – "In earnest." – "I am glad, for I don't like that kind of jesting."
additional_description: " A man asks another who has brushed against him: "Did you do that in earnest or in jest? " "In earnest. "  "I am glad, for I don't like that kind of jesting.

name: Sham threat: either ... or. "Either you give me the road or I (will give it to you, or the like)."
bibliographies: *Wesselski Hodscha Nasreddin II 21ff. No. 450; Lithuanian: Balys Index No. 1564*.

name: Sham threat: something he has never done before. Beggar says, "If you do not give me alms I shall have to do something I have never done before." The alms are given and he is asked what he would have had to do. "Work."
additional_description: Beggar says, "If you do not give me alms I shall have to do something I have never done before. " The alms are given and he is asked what he would have had to do. " Work.

name: Sham threat: the faked duel. Two who had challenged each other agree to hold a sham duel.
additional_description: Two who had challenged each other agree to hold a sham duel.

name: Sham threat: if I were not a philosopher I should break your head for you.
Girl's sham threat in order to evade husband till lover returns.
Sham threat of war holds ships back so that there suddenly are enough men to man defending ship.
Sham dream prophesying shipwreck makes people leave ship so that there is room enough for man who wants to go.
King menaced into giving his daughter by means of borrowed fleet.
Pretended anger.
King persuades men to follow him, pretending that he is going to make peace with his brothers, instead battle.
Bluff: insult repeated as harmless remark. The trickster makes an insulting remark, but when called on to repeat what he said he changes it so as to turn aside wrath.
bibliographies: Indonesia: DeVries's list No. 130; Africa (Basuto): Jacottet 12 No. 1, (Benga): Nassau 153ff. No. 19, (Yoruba): Ellis 266, (Ibo, Nigeria): Thomas 88, 151, (Kaffir): Theal 165, (Ila, Rhodesia): Smith and Dale II 384 No. 10, 395 No. 18; West Indies: Flowers 545. Cf. Chaucer Nun's Priest's Tale lines 343ff.
additional_description: The trickster makes an insulting remark, but when called on to repeat what he said he changes it so as to turn aside wrath.

name: Boast where the master cannot hear. The servant boasts that he has scolded his master.
additional_description: The servant boasts that he has scolded his master.

name: When he is looked at too threateningly hero feigns failing ability to go on horseback.
Threat to build a church in hell. When the man makes this threat, he is let out of hell.
bibliographies: Type 804*; Lithuanian: Balys Index No. 811A*; Estonian: Aarne FFC XXV No. 804*; Russian: Andrejev No. 804*.
additional_description: When the man makes this threat, he is let out of hell.

name: Bluff: wealth gained by seeming to be in the king's confidence. Courtier asks the king for a reward that will cost nothing. He gets permission to listen to the king's devotions. He now receives bribes because of his apparent influence.
bibliographies: *Penzer V 186 n. 1; *Wesselski Mönchslatein 127 No. 110, Morlini 266 No. 4; Clouston Tales II 360ff.; *Herbert III 421 No. 82; Scala Celi 136b No. 762.
additional_description: Courtier asks the king for a reward that will cost nothing. He gets permission to listen to the king's devotions. He now receives bribes because of his apparent influence.

name: Shoemaker offers to trim the peasant's feet to fit the shoes. The peasant prefers to accept the ill-fitting shoes.
additional_description: The peasant prefers to accept the ill-fitting shoes.

name: Herdsman threatens invasion with enormous herds: bought off. He hires himself as herdsman of all his master's flocks for ten years. He then sends notice to surrounding peoples that he is coming with his master's flocks to graze. They bribe him to stay away.
additional_description: He hires himself as herdsman of all his master's flocks for ten years. He then sends notice to surrounding peoples that he is coming with his master's flocks to graze. They bribe him to stay away.

bibliographies: India: Thompson-Balys: ASSAM HILLS . Rengma Naga : Mills 249 .
additional_description: Receives money to buy them off.

name: Adventurer on otherworld island sees great hornless oxen guarded by giant: tries to frighten them. "Why dost thou frighten the stilly calves?" says the huge herdsman. "Where are the dams of these calves?" asks the adventurer. "They are on the other side of yonder mountain," said he. So he went thence.
additional_description: Why dost thou frighten the stilly calves? " says the huge herdsman. " Where are the dams of these calves? " asks the adventurer. " They are on the other side of yonder mountain," said he. So he went thence.

name: Miracle must wait till one man is sacrificed. No one volunteers and it does not need to be performed.
additional_description: No one volunteers and it does not need to be performed.

name: Bluff: the rare vintages. Host serves many rare vintages and gets a reputation for his wine cellar. But there is only a small jug of each vintage.
additional_description: Host serves many rare vintages and gets a reputation for his wine cellar. But there is only a small jug of each vintage.

name: Man falsely claims to have killed elephant with his flat hand. Rewarded.
bibliographies: India: Thompson-Balys: ASSAM VALLEY . Barooah 113 No. 15 .
additional_description: Rewarded.

name: Fox threatens to catch bird, who feeds him her young as appeasement. He threatens to push down tree or to fly.
additional_description: He threatens to push down tree or to fly.

name: Sham duel in order to bring about recognition.
Feigned ignorance about person's identity in order to tell one's frank opinion of him.
Feigned ignorance of person's identity in order not to reveal king.
Feigned ignorance about the whereabouts of hero's weapons and horse in order to keep him as monk.
Illiterate man pretends to be weeping because he cannot make others understand the book he is reading.
Woman frightens robber away by telling him parrot's cry is husband's voice.

name: Overlooking the insult. Man consoles daughter whose husband is dying by telling her he has picked another husband for her. She feels insulted. No sooner does her husband die than she asks her father for details.

name: Villains and traitors.

Treacherous relatives. Distinction between treacherous relatives and cruel relatives (S0–S99) is frequently impossible to make. Relatives whose treachery seems to be uppermost have been listed here; those usually possessing power over their charges and exercising their power in a cruel fashion have been listed under cruel relatives.

name: Matron of Ephesus. (Vidua.) A woman mourns night and day by her husband's grave. A knight guarding a hanged man is about to lose his life because of the corpse he has stolen from the gallows. The matron offers him her love and substitutes her husband's corpse on the gallows so that the knight can escape.

Fatal game: jumping from tree. Lizard jumps from tree, plays dead. Rat jumps, really dies
Fatal flying contest: trickster (Maui) fatally tricks grandfather. Older man tries to fly; Maui flies under him, pulls his girdle and causes him to fall

name: Tossing game: trickster kills his opponent

Wife shoves husband over precipice by ruse: she brings coconut shell with very little water in it; as he leans far over to drink, she shoves him over edge
Person falls and is killed when using a cliffside latrine with improperly made supports
Person given a conch-horn of solid rock falls from tree and breaks neck when he tries to blow it
Dupe persuaded to climb on weakened limb; falls and dies

Invaders enticed one by one into a dark hut, where they are individually killed

name: Boy causes sharks to kill members of their own band by accusing them of collaborating with him

name: People caused to fight magic club in the dark

Raiding party stays far at sea during day, attacks stealthily each night
